one_line_takeaway: Freezing a pre-trained wav2vec 2.0 model and reading its EARLY-layer embeddings into a plain SVM matches near-state-of-the-art audio-spoofing detection (0.90% EER) with <1K trainable parameters on a CPU — a viable low-carbon alternative to fine-tuned deep back-ends.
key_finding: Best own system = 2nd transformer-layer embeddings + SVM (RBF kernel): 0.90% EER and 0.95 F1 with only 808 trainable parameters, all on CPU. This is competitive with the GPU-trained state-of-the-art AASIST (cited: 0.83% EER, 297K params). Averaged across all 13 layers, SVM gave the best mean EER (4.25%), ahead of MLP (4.37%) and logistic regression (4.87%). Using only up to the best (2nd) layer cuts the multiply-accumulate count by ~12 GMAC (~52%) and reduces stored parameters to ~19M (~80% less) versus the ~95M full wav2vec 2.0 BASE model.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a 'Green AI' (low-carbon, CPU-only) framework for audio deepfake / anti-spoofing detection. Rather than fine-tuning a speech foundation model and stacking a deep back-end (the prevailing 'Red AI' practice), the authors freeze an off-the-shelf pre-trained wav2vec 2.0 BASE model, extract frame-averaged 768-dim embeddings from its layers, and feed them to classical ML classifiers (SVM, logistic regression, MLP, plus KNN, Naive Bayes, decision tree). Two specific findings are the paper's own contribution: (1) competitive detection is achievable with under 1K trainable parameters trained entirely on a CPU, and (2) embeddings from an EARLY transformer layer (the 2nd/3rd) outperform the conventionally-used final layer for this task. Note: the strong AASIST results (0.83% EER) belong to prior cited work, not this paper — this paper's best own result is 0.90% EER.
threat_model: Audio deepfakes generated via text-to-speech (TTS) and voice conversion (VC) — 'audio deepfake technology' — used for identity theft and fraudulent impersonation, and as spoofing attacks against automatic speaker verification (ASV) systems. The proposed system is a countermeasure/detector that classifies utterances as bonafide vs. spoofed. Evaluated against the ASVspoof 2019 LA protocol, whose evaluation set contains 13 unseen TTS/VC attacks (vs. 6 attacks shared by train/dev), so it tests generalization to attacks not seen in training.
limitations: Evaluated on a single dataset (ASVspoof 2019 LA only) and a single SSL model (wav2vec 2.0 BASE); the authors themselves flag cross-dataset generalization and other foundation models (SUPERB-style) as future work. Their best EER (0.90%) is still slightly worse than the AASIST baseline (0.83%). The reported 'green' savings count only trainable/down-stream parameters and MAC of the truncated model — the frozen SSL feature extractor still must be stored (~19M params) and run at inference, so it is not free. Grid search used scikit-learn defaults unless specified, limiting hyperparameter exploration. The paper reports only single independent runs (no fusion/ensembling, no variance/CI). Code stated as to-be-released after acceptance.

datasets_used: ASVspoof 2019 Logical Access (LA) subset — 19 TTS/VC attacks; 6 attacks shared by train/dev, 13 unseen attacks in evaluation set
LibriSpeech 960h (English read speech) — used to pre-train the wav2vec 2.0 BASE SSL model (upstream, not fine-tuned here)
quantitative_results: Best own system (wav2vec 2.0 + SVM, 2nd transformer layer): 0.90% EER, 0.95 F1, 808 trainable parameters, CPU-only
Own wav2vec 2.0 + MLP: 1.11% EER, 77K trainable params, no GPU
Own wav2vec 2.0 + Logistic regression: 1.25% EER, 769 trainable params, no GPU
Average EER across 13 layers: SVM 4.25%, MLP 4.37%, logistic regression 4.87%
wav2vec 2.0 BASE compute: 23.04 GMACs vs LARGE 60.22 GMACs for ~3.5s audio
Green-AI savings using only up-to-2nd layer: ~12 GMAC (~52%) less compute; ~19M stored params (~80% less) vs ~95M full BASE model
Hardware: Intel Core i7-10700 CPU @ 2.90GHz, no GPU
baselines_compared: AASIST (cited): 0.83% EER, 297K params, GPU, raw audio
SENet (cited): 1.14% EER, 1,100K params, GPU, FFT front-end
LCNN-LSTM-sum (cited): 1.92% EER, 276K params, GPU, LFCC front-end
